summary: 64KB size limit does not protect against YAML bomb / alias expansion
detail: MaxConfigSize (64KB) is enforced before yaml.Unmarshal. YAML bombs use small input (e.g. 1KB) that expands to gigabytes during parsing via anchors/aliases. A malicious config under 64KB can cause OOM or excessive CPU during Unmarshal. gopkg.in/yaml.v3 supports anchors and aliases; CVE-2021-4235 (unbounded alias chasing) affected go-yaml—verify v3.0.1 includes the fix.
suggestion: Use yaml.Decoder with SetLimit or a custom decoder that caps node depth/alias count. Alternatively, reject YAML with anchors (e.g. scan for '&' and '*' before parse). Document that configs must come from trusted sources.

summary: gopkg.in/yaml.v3 v3.0.1: verify CVE-2021-4235 and CVE-2022-3064 applicability
detail: CVE-2021-4235 (unbounded alias chasing, CVSS 5.5) and CVE-2022-3064 (excessive CPU/memory) affect go-yaml. v3.0.1 fixes CVE-2022-28948 (panic). The v3 line shares code with v2; confirm v3.0.1 includes alias-chasing limits. Configs from untrusted sources (stdin, --config, gRPC) are parsed without additional constraints.
suggestion: Run go list -m -json gopkg.in/yaml.v3 and check for known vulnerabilities. Consider yaml.v3 fork with stricter limits if parsing untrusted input.

summary: loadFromArg accepts arbitrary paths; no restriction enables reading any file
detail: The --config flag and positional args are passed directly to os.Stat() and os.ReadFile() with no path sanitization or directory restriction. An attacker can pass ../../../etc/passwd or /home/user/.ssh/id_rsa to read arbitrary files. Content is parsed as YAML; invalid content yields parse errors, but the file is still read into memory.
suggestion: Restrict to a config directory, use filepath.Clean and filepath.EvalSymlinks, or require paths to be under a known base (e.g. current dir or XDG_CONFIG_HOME).

summary: cmd: values passed to shell without sanitization enable arbitrary command execution
detail: RunCommandOn passes the full command string to exec.Command("sh", "-c", cmdStr) or exec.Command("cmd", "/C", cmdStr). Config-controlled values from ResultActions and Button values are passed through. Validate() only checks that values start with cmd: and does not restrict shell metacharacters (;, |, $(), newlines, backticks). An attacker who supplies a config (--config file, stdin, or gRPC Notify) can execute arbitrary commands as the logged-in user.
suggestion: Validate cmd: values: reject newlines, and consider an allowlist of safe commands or exec with explicit argv (exec.Command(binary, args...) instead of shell). Document that only trusted configs should define cmd: actions.

summary: ResultActions values not validated for newlines; enables multi-command injection
detail: Validate() checks result_actions keys for newlines but not values. A value like "cmd:shutdown /r /t 60\nrm -rf /" passes validation. The newline is passed to sh -c and interpreted as a command separator, executing both commands.
suggestion: Add validation: if strings.HasPrefix(lower, "cmd:") && strings.ContainsAny(v, "\n\r") { errs = append(errs, ...) }

summary: loadFromArg only accepts inline config starting with '{'; block-style YAML rejected
detail: loadFromArg checks strings.HasPrefix(trimmed, "{") before treating arg as inline. Valid YAML like 'heading: Test\nmessage: Hi' is rejected with "not a file or JSON object". Config can still come from file or stdin. Error message says "JSON object" despite YAML support. Inconsistent UX; if block-style YAML is added, ensure same security checks apply.
suggestion: Accept inline YAML that doesn't start with '{' (e.g. try config.Load on trimmed bytes when file doesn't exist). Update error message. Apply same size/validation to all config sources.

summary: ResultActions allows http: URLs; enables open redirect and phishing
detail: Validate() permits result_actions values starting with http: (in addition to https:). When the user clicks a button or timeout triggers an action, url:http://evil.com opens in the browser. Images already restrict to https only; result_actions does not.
suggestion: Restrict result_actions url: values to https: only, consistent with Images validation.
